text: The patient is positioned in a semi-decubitus position, further tilted to an almost left lateral decubitus position, causing the spleen to fall medially. A retractor or instrument is used to lift and hang the spleen, facilitating the dissection of the splenic vessels.

The patient is positioned in a semi-decubitus position, further tilted to a left lateral decubitus position, causing the spleen to fall medially. A retractor or instrument is used to lift and hang the spleen, facilitating the dissection of splenic vessels. The surgeon enters the lesser sac by dividing the short gastric vessels from the midpoint of the greater curve to the upper pole of the spleen. The uppermost short gastric vessels are carefully divided, and the upper pole of the spleen is dissected away from the gastric fundus, with caution to avoid avulsion and bleeding.

The surgeon enters the lesser sac by dividing the short gastric vessels, starting from the midpoint of the greater curvature of the stomach up to the uppermost short gastric vessels near the upper pole of the spleen. The dissection is performed carefully to avoid avulsion of vessels, as the space is limited due to the large splenic lesion. The upper pole of the spleen is dissected away from the gastric fundus.

The surgeon decompresses the large splenic cyst to create space in the left upper quadrant, facilitating the procedure. The splenic artery is ligated to reduce the spleen's size, aiding in the dissection of splenic vessels. A hitching stitch is placed on the posterior wall of the stomach, securing it to the anterior abdominal wall near the lesser curve for improved exposure.

The surgeon decompresses the large splenic cyst to create space in the left upper quadrant. A hitching stitch is placed on the posterior wall of the stomach, securing it to the anterior abdominal wall to keep it away from the operative field. The short gastric vessels are divided, and the stomach is lifted for better exposure. The splenic vessels are identified at the upper border of the pancreas, and the peritoneum is opened at this location. The dissection of the splenic artery begins, with the artery ligated before the vein to facilitate spleen shrinkage and easier handling.

After dividing the short gastric vessels and lifting the stomach, the surgeon identifies the splenic vessels at the upper border of the pancreas. The peritoneum is opened at this location, and dissection of the splenic artery is initiated, prioritizing its ligation before the vein to facilitate spleen shrinkage.

The surgeon securely clips the splenic artery using hemlocks and divides it, observing the branching pattern of the artery which may include a single main branch or multiple polar branches. In some cases, the artery is clipped in continuity without division, and then retaken at the hilum on the splenic surface.

The surgeon clips and divides the splenic artery using hemlocks, ensuring secure ligation before commencing dissection of the splenic vein. The vein, dilated and thin-walled, is carefully ligated with a tie to prevent trauma, aided by the hanging maneuver that lifts the spleen in the lateral decubitus position. Multiple branches containing both artery and vein are clipped, divided, and ligated at the splenic hilum. The left gastroepiploic artery, a branch of the splenic artery, is divided near the splenic hilum to free the lower end of the spleen.

After completing the division of the splenic artery, the surgeon begins the dissection of the splenic vein. The vein appears dilated and thin-walled, with visible collaterals and branches entering the pancreas, requiring careful handling to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon clips, divides, and ligates multiple branches of the splenic artery and vein at the splenic hilum, bypassing dissection at the pancreatic bed due to difficulty in accessing the main splenic vein.

The surgeon performs a hanging maneuver with the patient in lateral decubitus position, lifting the spleen to facilitate dissection. The splenic vein is safely dissected and ligated with a tie to prevent trauma due to its dilated and thin-walled nature. Subsequently, a hemlock or clip is applied. The left gastroepiploic artery, a branch of the splenic artery near the splenic hilum, is divided to free the lower end of the spleen.

The surgeon identifies the splenic vein dividing into its tributaries and individually ligates each tributary for better venous control, minimizing the risk of clip slippage and bleeding. After dividing the splenic vein, the surgeon proceeds to separate the pancreas from the splenic hilum.

The surgeon individually ligates the tributaries of the splenic vein for better control, avoiding the confluence to reduce the risk of clip slippage and bleeding. After dividing the splenic vein, the surgeon proceeds to separate the pancreas from the splenic hilum.

The surgeon carefully dissects the pancreatic tail, which is deeply embedded in the splenic hilum, gently separating it from the surrounding tissue without damaging the pancreatic parenchyma. The spleen is suspended to maintain traction, aiding in the dissection process.

The surgeon carefully dissects the pancreatic tail from the splenic hilum, ensuring not to damage the pancreatic parenchyma or amputate the tail. The spleen is hung to maintain tissue traction, aiding in the gentle separation of the pancreatic tail from the splenic hilum.

The surgeon separates the short gastric vessels, splenic vessels, and pancreas from the splenic hilum, leaving the spleen suspended by the avascular linoleum ligament and diaphragmatic attachments. In cases of portal hypertension, inflammatory adhesions on the diaphragmatic surface are carefully divided.

The surgeon separates the short gastric vessels, splenic vessels, and pancreas from the splenic hilum, leaving the spleen suspended by the lienorenal ligament and diaphragmatic attachments. These attachments are divided, completing the splenectomy. Hemostasis is checked in the splenic bed and vascular stumps, and the spleen is retrieved through a suprapubic incision. A drain is placed in the splenic fossa to prevent seroma formation.
After completing the splenectomy, the surgeon checks hemostasis at the splenic bed and vascular stumps, ensuring no bleeding occurs. The spleen is then retrieved through a suprapubic incision.

A drain is placed in the splenic fossa to prevent seroma formation in the potential space, especially in cases with large spleens. The surgeon checks the drain fluid amylase before removing the drain.
